data.title: UnitedHealth drags Dow lower, Wall Street heads for weekly loss on tariff angst
data.description:
Wall Street on Thursday was mixed, as a post-earnings slump in industry bellwether UnitedHealth (UNH) was countered by some positive commentary from U.S. President Donald Trump and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent on tariffs. 

It is a holiday-shortened week, with markets shut on Good Friday.

The benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) was last +0.76% at 5,315.61 points in midday trade. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.26% at 16,348.82 points.

"We've had numerous talks at the highest level, frankly, with other countries. We're doing very well and we're moving along on trade very well. We're going to make fair deals," Trump told reporters at a White House event with Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni. Additionally, Bessent said that talks with Japan were moving in a "highly satisfactory" direction. 

The blue-chip Dow (DJI) remained under pressure, -0.73% at 39,379.43 points. Constituent UnitedHealth (UNH) crashed -22.3%, after the managed care giant delivered a rare quarterly bottom-line miss on soaring medical care costs. It also cut its full-year profit outlook. The performance reverberated across the health insurance sector, dragging peers such as Humana (HUM) -6.3% and Molina Healthcare (MOH) -2.2%.

Investors also received results from Dow 30 component American Express (AXP). All eyes will be on Netflix's (NFLX) results after the closing bell.

Earlier, monetary policy was in the spotlight after Trump criticized Federal Reserve chair Jerome Powell. Separately, the European Central Bank (ECB) cut interest rates for the seventh time in a year, and said that the outlook for growth had "deteriorated owing to rising trade tensions."  

Powell the previous day expressed concerns about the likely higher-than-anticipated effects from tariffs, including possibly putting pressure on policymakers' dual mandate of stable prices and strong employment. His hawkish comments spooked traders that were already reeling from a tech rout, leading to a steep selloff on Wall Street on Wednesday.

Trump on Thursday said Powell was "always TOO LATE AND WRONG" and that he "should have lowered Interest Rates, like the ECB, long ago." "Powell's termination cannot come fast enough!" the President added.

Politico reported that U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent had been privately underscoring that removing Powell as Fed chair would feed instability in markets, citing two people close to the White House.  

Over in the fixed-income markets, U.S. Treasury yields were to the upside. The benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y) was last up 3 basis points to 4.31%. The shorter-end more rate-sensitive 2-year yield (US2Y) was flat at 3.78%.

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

data.title: D.R. Horton projects Q3 revenue of $8.4B-$8.9B with 22,000-22,500 home closings
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: D.R. Horton (DHI) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Paul Romanowski highlighted earnings of $2.58 per diluted share and revenues of $7.7 billion for Q2 2025, with a pre-tax profit margin of 13.8%. He emphasized the company's focus on balancing pace and price to adapt to affordability constraints and market conditions. Homes sales gross margin was 21.8%, with 58% of Q2 closings sold in the same quarter.
  • CFO Bill Wheat noted that the company repurchased 9.7 million shares, totaling $1.3 billion in Q2, and declared a quarterly dividend of $0.40 per share. He also mentioned $5.8 billion in consolidated liquidity as of March 31.
  • EVP Michael Murray reported that average closing prices were $372,500, a 1% decline year-over-year and sequentially. Home sales revenue was $7.2 billion on 19,276 homes closed, while net sales orders decreased 15% year-over-year.

Outlook

  • Management expects Q3 2025 consolidated revenues of $8.4 billion to $8.9 billion, with home closings projected between 22,000 to 22,500 units. Home sales gross margin is anticipated to range from 21% to 21.5%, with pre-tax profit margins expected to be 13.3% to 13.8%.
  • Full-year 2025 guidance includes consolidated revenues of $33.3 billion to $34.8 billion, and home closings of 85,000 to 87,000 units. The company plans to repurchase approximately $4 billion of its stock in fiscal 2025.

Financial Results

  • Net income for Q2 was reported at $810 million, with 22,437 net sales orders and an order value of $8.4 billion. The cancellation rate was 16%, slightly improved sequentially but higher year-over-year.
  • Homebuilding SG&A expenses increased by 4%, representing 8.9% of revenues. The average number of active selling communities rose 10% year-over-year.
  • Rental operations generated $23 million in pre-tax income on $237 million in revenues, reflecting a focus on capital efficiency through pre-leasing sales of rental properties.

Q&A

  • Stephen Kim from Evercore ISI asked about the company's shift from volume-focused metrics to consistent operating cash flow. CEO Romanowski stated that the focus remains on balancing pace and price to maximize returns.
  • Alan Ratner from Zelman & Associates highlighted concerns about spec home inventory and future growth. Romanowski explained that improved cycle times allow for lower inventory levels while meeting market demands efficiently.
  • John Lovallo from UBS inquired about Q3 gross margin guidance sensitivity to sales incentives. COO Michael Murray confirmed that incentives could influence margins but stressed adaptability to market conditions.
  • Michael Rehaut from JPMorgan questioned the impact of tariffs on costs. Romanowski noted that while there is uncertainty, the company's scale positions it to absorb potential cost pressures effectively.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns about market affordability constraints, inventory levels, and gross margin pressures. Questions were targeted at understanding the company's adaptability to uncertain economic conditions.
  • Management's tone remained confident during prepared remarks, with a strong emphasis on operational efficiency and shareholder returns. In Q&A, responses were measured, highlighting adaptability and focus on returns.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts appeared slightly more cautious, reflecting broader market uncertainties and rising incentive costs.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 guidance indicated lower gross margins compared to Q1, driven by higher sales incentives and affordability pressures. Home sales revenues and closings were slightly lower compared to Q1.
  • The share repurchase strategy intensified in Q2, with $1.3 billion spent on buybacks, compared to $1.1 billion in Q1.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from growth potential to operational resilience amid affordability challenges.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged continued affordability constraints and economic uncertainty, particularly regarding mortgage interest rates and consumer confidence.
  • Analysts raised questions about the impact of tariffs, land inflation, and potential SG&A leverage challenges.
  • Rising sales incentives to maintain traffic and conversions could pressure future gross margins.

Final Takeaway

D.R. Horton delivered solid Q2 results amid challenging market conditions, emphasizing operational efficiency and shareholder returns. The company projects increased revenues and home closings in Q3, supported by improved cycle times and strong inventory management. Despite affordability constraints and rising incentive costs, management remains confident in its ability to adapt and sustain market leadership.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: UnitedHealth plunges 23% and its fall sent ripples across the ETF landscape
data.description:
Shares of UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (NYSE:UNH) took a sharp dive on Thursday after the healthcare giant slashed its full-year profit guidance in response to underwhelming first-quarter earnings.

The disappointing results triggered a swift market reaction, with the stock tumbling 23.1% to $451.60 by mid-morning trading—briefly touching a low of $448, its weakest level since February 21. 

The earnings miss sent ripples across the broader healthcare investment landscape, weighing heavily on exchange traded funds with significant exposure to the stock. UnitedHealth is currently held within 392 ETFs, representing a combined 139.1 million shares. The broad selloff underscores the stock’s outsized influence in healthcare-focused portfolios and major index funds. 

No. 10: ProShares Ultra Health Care (NYSEARCA:RXL), 8.20% allocation.

No. 9: Global X Dow 30 Covered Call & Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:DYLG), 8.88% allocation.

No. 8: SPDR Dow Jones Industrial Average ETF (NYSEARCA:DIA), 8.90% allocation.

No. 7: Global X Dow 30 Covered Call ETF (NYSEARCA:DJIA), 8.90% allocation.

No. 6: Future Health Care Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:GDOC), 9.19% allocation.

No. 5: Fidelity MSCI Health Care Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FHLC), 9.39% allocation.

No. 4: JPMorgan Healthcare Leaders ETF (NASDAQ:JDOC), 9.51% allocation.

No. 3: iShares U.S. Healthcare ETF (NYSEARCA:IYH), 10.48% allocation. 

No. 2: Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSEARCA:XLV), 10.70% allocation. 

No. 1: iShares U.S. Healthcare Providers ETF (NYSEARCA:IHF), 25.36% allocation. 

On a Q1 earnings basis, UNH posted Non-GAAP EPS of $7.20 which missed estimates by $0.09. At the same time the company provided quarterly revenue of $109.58B (+9.8% Y/Y) which missed the forecasted consensus mark by $2.02B.

data.title: SA analyst upgrades: AMD, PLTR, VKTX, HUBS, MO, USB, SFIX, VERV
data.description:
Here are the latest rating upgrades from Seeking Alpha analysts:

Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD). Hold to buy by Kenio Fontes. "The Company's forward P/E of ~19x for 2025 EPS offers a margin of safety, prompting an upgrade in the stock rating…While cautious of industry cyclicality and competition, AMD (NASDAQ:AMD) presents an asymmetric investment opportunity with potential for significant upside and improved fundamentals."

Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR). Sell to hold by Oakoff Investments.  "Despite high valuation metrics, the long-term growth potential and strategic importance of Palantir's offerings justify at least a "Hold" rating for Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR) stock, with potential for future upgrades."

Viking Therapeutics (NASDAQ:VKTX). Hold to buy by Juxtaposed Ideas. "Combined with the richer balance sheet, relatively efficient operational efforts, and soon-to-commence clinical Phase 3 trials, the stock is likely to outperform from current discounted levels."

HubSpot (NYSE:HUBS). Sell to buy by Gary Alexander. "Despite potential tariff-related headwinds, HubSpot's (NYSE:HUBS) conservative guidance and currency tailwinds provide a buffer, making it a good buy at current levels."

Altria (NYSE:MO). Hold to buy by Dividendology. "Management is pivoting towards a smoke-free future, investing in smoke-free products to sustain long-term growth. Valuation models indicate potential upside with fair values around $65, making Altria (NYSE:MO) a solid high-yield investment option."

U.S. Bancorp (NYSE:USB). Hold to buy by Dividend and Value Investor. "I believe that USB (NYSE:USB) shares look increasingly attractive with a long-term investment horizon. The first quarter results published today have strengthened my confidence in the bank's financial stability."

Stitch Fix (NASDAQ:SFIX). Sell to hold by Gary Alexander. "Despite ongoing risks, including potential tariffs, Stitch Fix's (NASDAQ:SFIX) ~4x adjusted EBITDA multiple (alongside meaningful adjusted EBITDA expansion) suggests it may be nearing a price floor."

Verve Therapeutics (NASDAQ:VERV). Hold to buy by Myriam Alvarez. "Verve's (NASDAQ:VERV) VERVE-102 showed an impressive 69% LDL-C reduction in its early Phase 1b data…I think VERV remains a high-risk, long-term play in gene editing. But its risk-reward profile now seems to justify a “Buy” rating."

data.title: Tariffs drive nearly one-quarter of Americans to scrap major purchases - Redfin
data.description:
Some 24% of Americans are abandoning plans for buying big-ticket items like homes or cars due to President Donald Trump's tariffs, while 32% are just holding off, according to a Redfin (RDFN) survey released Thursday.

With tariffs stoking fears of higher inflation and recession, “consumers are tightening their belts because they are rightly nervous about their job security and the prospect of paying more for everyday expenses," said Redfin Economist Lead Chen Zhao.

Further underscoring consumers' caution, more than half (55%) of survey respondents said Trump's tariff policies made them less inclined to make major purchases this year. By comparison, only 13% said they were more likely to make a major purchase because of the tariffs.

For the housing market, in particular, the new tariffs are already lowering homebuying demand and likely will significantly raise construction costs, online brokerage Redfin said. 

On current conditions, 34% of respondents reported they do not have an emergency fund to cover their monthly mortgage or rent payments in the event they face a financial crisis, like losing a job. But half of them do have one.

The Redfin-commissioned survey was conducted by Ipsos between April 10-14, 2025 and fielded to 1,004 U.S. adults.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Mortgage rates rise but purchase application demand remains solid
data.description:
Mortgage rates rose this week but remained below 7%, while purchase application demand remained strong, according to the latest Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Survey.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.83% as of April 17, higher than 6.62% the prior week but lower than 7.10% in the year-ago week.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.03%, compared to 5.82% a week ago and 6.39% a year ago.

"The 30-year fixed-rate mortgage ticked up but remains below the 7% threshold for the thirteenth consecutive week," said Freddie Mac's chief economist, Sam Khater.

"At this time last year, rates reached 7.1%, while purchase application demand was 13% lower than it is today, a clear sign that this year’s spring homebuying season is off to a stronger start," added Khater.

data.title: Philadelphia Fed names Anna Paulson to succeed Harker as president
data.description:
The Federal Reserve Bank of Philadelphia named Anna Paulson as its next president and CEO, succeeding Patrick Harker, whose term ends June 30, 2025, the district reserve bank said on Thursday.

Paulson is currently executive vice president and director of research at the Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago. She'll take office on July 1, 2025, the Philly Fed said.

She has 20 years of experience in policy analysis and research related to financial stability and monetary policy. As executive vice president and director of research at the Chicago Fed, Paulson advises the bank's president and CEO on monetary policy and attends Federal Open Market Committee meetings.

Following the rotation schedule of district Fed bank presidents, The Philly Fed president will next be a  voting member of the FOMC in 2026.

data.title: Fed's Barr urges banks to adopt AI to combat rising deepfake risks
data.description:
Federal Reserve Governor Michael Barr on Thursday urged banks to adopt AI-powered tools, such as facial recognition, voice analysis and behavioral biometrics, to enhance customer identity verification and counter growing deepfake risks.

A deepfake is a fake piece of digital content created with AI to ultimately replicate a real person's identity.

Such technology "has the potential to supercharge identity fraud," Barr warned in remarks prepared for delivery at the Federal Reserve Bank of New York.

His solution: "we should take steps to lessen the impact of attacks by making successful breaches less likely, while making each attack more resource-intensive for the attacker."

The Fed's former top banking cop noted that lenders already have dual oversight of transactions, verifying both the sender's identity and the recipient's address. In addition to "employing advanced analytics to flag suspicious patterns that could indicate fraudulent activities," banks are already "engaging with other financial institutions to help define the threat and identify appropriate controls and mitigants," he added.

It's also worth exploring how regulators can use AI to "enhance our ability to monitor and detect patterns of fraudulent activity at regulated institutions in real time," he said. This could help provide early warnings to affected institutions and broader industry participants, as well as to protect our own systems."

Earlier this month, Bloomberg reported that hackers spied on the emails of around 100 banking regulators at the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency for more than a year, infiltrating messages comprising agency deliberations and sensitive details about the banks it oversees.

data.title: Unitedhealth group revises FY2025 EPS outlook to $26-$26.50 amid higher care activity
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: UnitedHealth Group (UNH) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Andrew Witty reported that the company observed "continued strong growth across our businesses," but also noted "an overall performance that was frankly unusual and unacceptable." The adjusted earnings per share outlook for the year has been revised to $26 to $26.50.
  • Higher-than-expected care activity in UnitedHealthcare's Medicare Advantage business during Q1 2025, particularly in physician and outpatient services, was a significant factor. This increase was limited to the Medicare Advantage segment and did not affect commercial or Medicaid businesses.
  • An influx of new Medicare patients at Optum Health, particularly from plans exiting markets and with lower-than-expected reimbursement levels, was highlighted as an issue impacting revenue. These challenges were compounded by CMS risk model changes.
  • Key initiatives include enhancing member engagement, improving the transition to the new CMS risk model, and updating Medicare Advantage plan designs for 2026.
  • CFO John Rex emphasized that "we now expect adjusted earnings of $26 to $26.50 per share," reflecting higher care activity and changes in patient profiles. Revenue guidance remains at $450 billion to $455 billion.
  • Operating earnings outlooks for UnitedHealthcare and Optum Health have been adjusted, with specific impacts attributed to care activity and patient mix changes.

Outlook

  • Full-year medical care ratio is expected to be 87.5%, reflecting increased care activity and patient mix changes.
  • Revenue outlook for Optum Health is set at $106 billion to $107 billion, with operating earnings projected between $6.2 billion to $6.4 billion.
  • UnitedHealthcare's operating earnings outlook has been revised to $16 billion to $16.5 billion.
  • Plans for 2026 Medicare Advantage include addressing the trends observed in 2025 and incorporating them into pricing and plan designs.

Financial Results

  • The company reported care activity in Q1 2025 at twice the expected rate, particularly in the Medicare Advantage business.
  • Optum Rx revenues grew by 14% to exceed $35 billion in the quarter, supported by customer retention and new wins.
  • Consolidated revenue remains consistent with previous guidance, at $450 billion to $455 billion for the full year.
  • Adjusted earnings per share guidance was lowered to $26 to $26.50 for FY2025, reflecting changes in care activity and patient profiles.

Q&A

  • Justin Lake, Wolfe Research, inquired about the Medicare Advantage cost trend and the company’s assumptions. Tim Noel responded, explaining the doubling of care activity in Q1 2025 and the assumption that it will persist throughout the year and into 2026.
  • Josh Raskin, Nephron Research, questioned the connection between higher primary care visits and Optum Health's pressure. Dr. Amar Desai elaborated on the challenges from new Medicare patients and the CMS risk model transition.
  • Stephen Baxter, Wells Fargo, asked about Medicare Advantage margins and the timeline for recovery. Tim Noel stated that margins are expected to remain within targeted ranges for 2025.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns over unexpected increases in care activity and its implications for margins and future guidance. Questions were critical yet constructive, seeking clarity on management’s actions.
  • Management showed a combination of cautious optimism and determination to address the challenges, emphasizing the temporary nature of current issues and confidence in long-term strategies.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone was more defensive, reflecting the unexpected performance challenges in Q1 2025.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Adjusted EPS guidance has been revised down from the initial outlook provided in Q4 2024.
  • Significant shifts in care activity trends and patient profiles were identified in Q1 2025, contrasting with a more stable outlook in Q4 2024.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from general performance to specific impacts of Medicare Advantage trends and Optum Health’s challenges.
  • Management’s tone in Q1 2025 was more focused on addressing performance shortfalls, compared to a more forward-looking and optimistic tone in Q4 2024.

Risks and Concerns

  • Increased care activity in Medicare Advantage beyond expectations poses a risk to profitability.
  • Challenges in transitioning to the new CMS risk model impacted revenue and operational efficiency.
  • Medicaid funding adequacy and Medicare Advantage pricing adjustments remain areas of focus.
  • Potential regulatory changes in pharmacy benefit management could impact Optum Rx operations.

Final Takeaway

UnitedHealth Group highlighted both significant challenges and actions to address them in Q1 2025. Higher-than-expected care activity and changes in patient profiles have led to a revision in EPS guidance. Despite these headwinds, management emphasized its confidence in addressing these issues and improving performance into 2026. Investors should monitor the execution of these strategies and the impact of ongoing changes in Medicare Advantage and Optum Health operations.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: DoE preparing $10B clean energy funding cuts which could hurt Occidental, Exxon projects - WSJ
data.description:
The U.S. Department of Energy is preparing dramatic cuts that could halt nearly $10B in federal funding for clean energy projects including hydrogen, carbon capture, long-duration energy storage and other technologies, The Wall Street Journal reported Thursday.

Among government contracts at risk of losing DoE funding are those with Exxon Mobil (XOM) to use hydrogen for fuel at a large ethylene plant, Occidental Petroleum (NYSE:OXY) for carbon capture in south Texas, and NextEra Energy (NEE) for long duration storage, according to the report.

The DoE also is said to be considering withdrawing funding from four regional hydrogen hubs, largely in Democratic-leaning regions, while continuing to help fund three hydrogen hubs in largely Republican areas, and more than 250 projects tied to electric vehicle charging, wave energy, battery recycling, solar, wind and other technologies also are reportedly at risk.

The cuts are part of a broad campaign by President Trump's Department of Government Efficiency to shrink the scale of the government and its spending, which already has resulted in thousands of job cuts across the federal workforce.

Most of the DoE's grants and loans have been in limbo since February, when Trump signed an executive order pausing the release of federal funds appropriated under the Infrastructure Improvement and Jobs Act and the Inflation Reduction Act that were approved during the Biden administration.
